coredata.dc:title: Lignin-derived carbon quantum dot/PVA films for totally blocking UV and high-energy blue light
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Seok Won
Im, So Hui
Hong, Woo Tae
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
Jung, Yun Kyung
coredata.dc:description: Excessive exposure to UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) can cause fatal eye and skin injuries. As a result, it is crucial to protect our bodies from UV and HEBL radiation. To achieve complete blocking of UV and HEBL, we developed a lignin-derived carbon quantum dot (L-CQD)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film. L-CQD was synthesized from lignin, a waste woody biomass, and then blended with a PVA matrix to create a flexible L-CQD/PVA film. Thanks to simultaneous UV and HEBL absorption characteristics and bright color of L-CQD, the PVA film with 0.375 wt% L-CQD demonstrated outstanding blocking efficiency: 100 % in UV-C, UV-B, and UV-A, and at least 99.9 % in HEBL. It also exhibited a 44 % increase in lightness and a 12 % enhancement in transparency compared to lignin/PVA film. The film's ability to block UV and HEBL was further demonstrated by reducing >40 % UV-induced ROS formation in both cancerous and normal cell lines (Hs 294T, HeLa, CCD-986sk, and L929), as well as by blocking blue laser diode (LD) and LED. Since the L-CQD/PVA film is simple to produce, environmentally friendly, flexible, and thermally stable, it is suitable for use as a protective coating against sunlight and harmful emissions from IT devices.

head.ce:title: Lignin-derived carbon quantum dot/PVA films for totally blocking UV and high-energy blue light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive exposure to UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) can cause fatal eye and skin injuries. As a result, it is crucial to protect our bodies from UV and HEBL radiation. To achieve complete blocking of UV and HEBL, we developed a lignin-derived carbon quantum dot (L-CQD)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film. L-CQD was synthesized from lignin, a waste woody biomass, and then blended with a PVA matrix to create a flexible L-CQD/PVA film. Thanks to simultaneous UV and HEBL absorption characteristics and bright color of L-CQD, the PVA film with 0.375 wt% L-CQD demonstrated outstanding blocking efficiency: 100 % in UV-C, UV-B, and UV-A, and at least 99.9 % in HEBL. It also exhibited a 44 % increase in lightness and a 12 % enhancement in transparency compared to lignin/PVA film. The film's ability to block UV and HEBL was further demonstrated by reducing >40 % UV-induced ROS formation in both cancerous and normal cell lines (Hs 294T, HeLa, CCD-986sk, and L929), as well as by blocking blue laser diode (LD) and LED. Since the L-CQD/PVA film is simple to produce, environmentally friendly, flexible, and thermally stable, it is suitable for use as a protective coating against sunlight and harmful emissions from IT devices.

ce:keyword.ce:text: UV and high-energy blue light-blocking film

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation, a sort of electromagnetic radiation between visible light and X-rays, is usually divided into three categories based on wavelength: UV-C (200–280 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm), and UV-A (315–400 nm) [1]. Exposure to UV radiation that is naturally emitted by the sun significantly impacts human health [2]. While moderate UV exposure helps produce vitamin D, excessive UV radiation can damage skin and eyes, leading to photoaging, skin cancer, cataracts, and luteal degeneration [3–6]. Especially, UV-B is known to induce the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS can cause oxidative stress, cell damage, and apoptosis [2,7,8]. Additionally, the widespread use of electronic devices like cell phones, tablets, and light-emitting diode (LED) televisions has led to increased exposure to high-energy blue light (HEBL) [9]. HEBL has the shortest and highest energy waves in the visible spectrum, ranging from 400 to 450 nm [10–12]. Overexposure to HEBL disrupts sleep patterns by inhibiting the secretion of melatonin, a sleep-inducing hormone. It can also damage the retina, potentially increase ROS production, and cause the destruction of photoreceptor and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells, leading to apoptosis [13,14]. To address these issues, it is necessary to develop a film that selectively filters UV and HEBL rays while maintaining visible transparency.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a water-soluble synthetic polymer that is biodegradable under both aerobic and anaerobic conditions. It is stable in almost all solvents and has several advantages, including high resistance to oil and chemicals, non-toxicity, excellent optical transparency, and high flexibility [15,16]. Because of these properties, PVA was chosen as the matrix material for the film fabrication in this study.

Lignin, a naturally occurring polymer derived from wood waste biomass, has inherent UV-absorbing properties due to the presence of functional groups such as phenolic units, ketones, and other UV chromophores [17]. It is recognized as a renewable and promising alternative to synthetic UV protection agents. However, its inherent darkness restricts its use to lignin-based natural sunscreens by reducing the product's transparency. Although there have been various UV-blocking films developed, including nanolignin/PVA films, ZrO2/PVA films, xylan/TiO2-PVA films, lignin/cellulose films, carbon dot/cellulose films, and cellulose nanocrystal-diethyl ferulate (CNC-DEF)/PVA films, these face challenges such as low visible light transmittance, complex manufacturing processes, and the inability to completely block both UV and HEBL at the same time [18–23].

In this study, we synthesized lignin-derived carbon quantum dot (L-CQD)/PVA films to simultaneously block UV and HEBL regions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical particles <10 nm in size with fluorescence emission properties, minimal toxicity, great biocompatibility, photostability, and ease of surface modification with diverse functional groups [24,25]. These properties have made CQDs increasingly popular in bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensors, antibacterial agents, and nanozyme applications [24–28]. Our technology introduces new developments for CQD-based UV and HEBL-blocking films. Firstly, to mitigate lignin's intrinsic dark color and limited transparency, we synthesized L-CQDs from lignin, a carbon source, with ethylenediamine (EDA) as a passivating agent in an alkaline ethanol solution through hydrothermal synthesis (Scheme 1a). This synthesis produced L-CQDs with an absorption peak beyond 400 nm and a brighter color than standard lignin solutions. These improvements resulted from enhanced passivation of surface traps and a redshift in the absorption spectrum, achieved by employing amino group-containing EDA and an alkaline solution [29–32]. Consequently, L-CQDs exhibited simultaneous UV and HEBL absorption properties. Secondly, utilizing these optical characteristics, we developed an L-CQD-embedded PVA film (L-CQD/PVA film) through a straightforward mixing and drying process (Scheme 1b). The resulting L-CQD/PVA film showed increased optical transparency across the visible light spectrum while entirely blocking UV and HEBL transmission. To our knowledge, no previous study has reported on L-CQD-embedded films that can completely block both UV and HEBL. Lastly, we evaluated the performance of the L-CQD/PVA film by testing its effectiveness in reducing UV-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and blue light exposure (Scheme 1c). The L-CQD/PVA film demonstrated superior efficacy in suppressing UV-induced ROS generation in both cancerous and normal cell lines (Hs 294T, HeLa, CCD-986sk, and L929) compared to samples without any film or those with a PVA film. Moreover, the film's ability to block HEBL increased with higher L-CQD concentrations when tested with blue laser diode (LD) and LED light sources.

Sodium hydroxide, ethylenediamine (EDA) and 2′,7’-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCF-DA) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Lignin (Alkaline) was supplied by Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). The alkaline lignin was derived from needle-leaved and broad-leaved trees through a series of chemical modifications including sodium sulfite treatment, partial desulfonation, oxidation, hydrolysis, demethylation, and pH adjustment within the alkaline range of 8.0–10.0. PVA was purchased from Samchun Chemicals (Pyeongtaek, Korea). Ethyl alcohol was bought from SK Chemicals (Seongnam, Korea). A human melanoma skin cell line (Hs 294T) and a human dermal fibroblast cell line (CCD-986sk) were obtained from the Korea Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea). RPMI 1640 medium and fetal bovine serum were acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Massachusetts, MA, USA). Life technologies (Carlsbad, CA, USA) provided penicillin-streptomycin and trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lignin-derived carbon quantum dot (L-CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: L-CQD was produced through hydrothermal pyrolysis. 110 mg of lignin and 1320 μL of EDA were dissolved in 15 mL of 0.05 % NaOH solution and ethanol solution (1:1 vol/vol) and mixed thoroughly using sonication. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon vessel and deposited in an autoclave container made of stainless steel for hydrothermal reaction. The solution was heated to 200 °C for 12 h and cooled naturally to room temperature. The unreacted carbon residue was removed by filtration using a 0.2 μm membrane filter (CHMLAB group, Spain), and the product was further purified using silica gel (70–230 mesh) column chromatography. Then, the filtered solution was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to remove surplus solution and used to produce the PVA-containing film for UV and HEBL blocking. The dried L-CQD was readily dissolved in 1 mL of 0.05 % NaOH:ethanol solution (1:1 vol/vol) at different concentrations (0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt%) and subjected to thorough sonication for film preparation.

PVA (MW = 500 g/mol) was used as a matrix to form a film containing L-CQD. A transparent PVA solution was made by adding 1 g of PVA powder to 20 mL of deionized water and stirring at 75 °C for 1 h. After cooling slowly to room temperature, 3 mL of PVA solution was poured into the beaker. By adding L-CQD solutions of varying concentrations to the PVA solution and stirring vigorously, a well-mixed solution was created. The solution was drop-cast into a petri dish and dried at 60 °C for a day. L-CQD/PVA films with different amounts of L-CQD were produced. As a control, a PVA film without L-CQD was also prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV/HEBL blocking performance of PVA film and L-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: The UV and HEBL absorption capabilities of each L-CQD/PVA film were investigated by UV–vis spectrophotometry. Using optical transmittance, the UV and HEBL blocking efficacy of L-CQD/PVA films was evaluated. The UV and HEBL protection percentages of the L-CQD/PVA films were calculated using the following equation [33].

The UV-A (315–400 nm) blocking rate of each film was calculated using Eq. (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of UV-induced ROS production in cells using L-CQD/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Inhibitory effect of L-CQD/PVA films on UV-induced ROS generation in cells was examined using a ROS detection cell-based assay kit. After seeding 1 × 104 cells/well (Hs 294T melanoma cells, CCD-986sk normal cells, HeLa cancer cells, and L929 normal cells) in a black 96-well plate, they were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 condition. Then, each well was exposed to 254 nm and 365 nm UV radiation for 5 min and incubated again for 24 h. After adding 200 μL of 10 μM DCF-DA solution, the incubation was performed for 30 min, and the UV-induced ROS production rate of the cells was assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and an emission wavelength of 520 nm after 15 min using a cell imaging multimode reader (Cytation5, BioTek, USA). The cell viability test followed the same procedure as the ROS test. Following a 5-min exposure to UV light, each well was subsequently incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were treated with WST-1 (water-soluble tetrazolium 1) solution and their viability was determined by measuring the absorbance intensity at 450 nm using a cell imaging multimode reader (Synergy HT, BioTek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blue LD and blue LED blocking performance of L-CQD/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effectiveness of L-CQD/PVA film for blocking blue light, a blue LD (< 200 mW, 405 nm) was used to illuminate a black wall. Under blue LD irradiation, photographs were taken with PVA film and L-CQD/PVA films containing different L-CQD concentrations (0.125, 0.25, and 0.375 wt%). Furthermore, blue light blocking performance was evaluated using a blue LED (5 W, 450 nm). PVA film and L-CQD/PVA film of various concentrations were covered on the blue LED to obtain pictures of the films. Then, the transmitted light for each spectrum of each film was measured with a photodetector (SM245, Korea Spectral Products, Korea).

To develop a unique CQD with the ability to block UV and HEBL, we used dark-hued lignin as the carbon source and EDA as the nitrogen passivation agent [34] in a NaOH:ethanol solution (1:1). Lignin possesses UV chromophore functional groups, such as quinones and methoxy-substituted phenoxy groups, enabling it to effectively absorb a wide variety of wavelengths within the UV spectrum, particularly in the 250–400 nm region [17,35]. The synthesized L-CQD solution in water appeared faint yellow in natural light, but when subjected to irradiation from a 365 nm UV lamp, it displayed blue-green fluorescence (inset of Fig. 1a). The quantum yield (QY) of the L-CQD was calculated to be 19.61 ± 3.29 %. The absorbance spectrum of the L-CQD solution shows a shoulder peak at 273 nm due to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC (Fig. 1a) [36]. The appearance of a second faint absorption peak at 350 nm can be attributed to the n → π* transition of the CO functional group [37]. It also showed a pronounced absorption peak at 420 nm, which is responsible for the yellow hue of the L-CQDs generated from the o-quinone of lignin [38]. The surface defect sites on CQDs can operate as electron and hole traps, limiting their recombination and photoluminescence efficiency. The amine groups of EDA passivate the surface traps on the L-CQDs. This process facilitates electron-hole radiation recombination, resulting in increased photon emission and photoluminescence [29–31]. Moreover, the alkalinity of the alkaline ethanol solution creates an electron-rich environment on the surface of the CQDs, which can elevate the HOMO to higher energy levels and decrease the bandgap, resulting in a redshift of the absorption and fluorescence [32]. The increased passivation of surface traps and a redshift in absorption are believed to be responsible for the simultaneous and intense absorption of the L-CQDs in the UV and HEBL areas. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the L-CQDs exhibited a maximum emission at 510 nm when subjected to excitation at a wavelength of 410 nm (Fig. 1b). Moreover, upon stimulation within the wavelength range of 370–430 nm, excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior was demonstrated, with an emission wavelength change from 505 nm to 515 nm. As a result of this phenomenon, the L-CQD exhibits a shift in emitted light from blue to green [39]. It appears that L-CQDs exhibit the potential to alleviate the adverse impacts of blue light through their efficient absorption of photons within the blue light spectrum. From the optical investigations, the successful synthesis of L-CQDs with the dual capability of absorbing both UV and HEBL was achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical analysis of L-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, particle size distribution, and crystal structure of L-CQD were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 2a and Fig. S1 depict the uniformly dispersed, spherical shape of the L-CQDs, with an average diameter of 8.36 ± 0.26 nm and a size range between 5 and 12 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2a, inset) [40]. DLS analysis further confirmed the size of the L-CQDs, with an average diameter of 8.45 ± 0.13 nm (Fig. S2), aligning with the TEM results. The XRD pattern of the synthesized L-CQD shows two broad diffraction peaks centered at 2θ = 25.5° and 2θ = 41.5°, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes with d-spacings of 0.34 nm and 0.217 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b) [41–43].

To identify the functional groups on the surface of L-CQD, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken (Fig. 2c). The broad and strong peaks observed in the range of 3600–3000 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds [40]. Specifically, the absorption peak at 3388 cm−1 can be linked to the –NH groups [31]. The peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds [44]. The peak at 1590 cm−1 is also ascribed to –NH groups, suggesting the incorporation of EDA [31]. Furthermore, the peaks at 1695, 1422, and 1134 cm-1 are due to the stretching of CO, the bending of CH in CH3, and the stretching of CC in the ring structure, respectively, which may also be indicative of the presence of lignin [16,45,46]. The vibration at 1350 cm−1 is associated with the CN vibration, which may be attributed to EDA used as a passivating agent [47]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was used to study the atomic composition of L-CQD. The XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 2d indicates peaks for C1s, O1s, S2p, N1s, and Na1s, with atomic percentages of 58.0 %, 25.5 %, 1.1 %, 6.6 %, and 8.8 %, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S3) offer further insights into their specific functional groups. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S3a) exhibits peaks at 287.6 eV, 285.1 eV, and 284.0 eV, which correspond to CO, CN, and C-C/C=C, respectively [34,48]. The peaks observed at 535.6 eV, 532.3 eV, and 530.8 eV in Fig. S3b are linked to OH, C-O/C=O, and CO functional groups, respectively [49–51]. The S2p spectrum represents the energy levels for sulfate, S2p1/2, and S2p3/2, from lignin, at 168.8 eV, 167.5 eV, and 163.1 eV, respectively (Fig. S3c) [52–54]. Furthermore, a binding energy of 398.8 eV in Fig. S3d is associated with the NC bond, which arises from nitrogen-doping of EDA [55–57]. The Na+ ions present in the solvent are associated with a binding energy of 1070.6 eV (Fig. S3e) [58]. Also, the zeta potential analysis was employed to ascertain the surface charge of L-CQD (Fig. S4). The zeta potential of L-CQD decreased from 24.38 ± 0.31 mV to −43.25 ± 0.13 mV as the pH shifted from 2 to 11. The positive charge is likely due to the presence of EDA, while the negative charge is probably due to NaOH. The identification of sulfur- and nitrogen-related functional groups in the FTIR and XPS data, along with the surface charge findings from the zeta potential analysis, confirms that L-CQD was successfully synthesized using lignin and EDA.

The novel L-CQD was designed to mitigate the naturally dark brown color of lignin (Fig. S5). At the same concentration, a lignin solution has a darker color and a higher absorbance at 550 nm than an L-CQD solution, indicating lower transparency in visible light. PVA films were fabricated using varying concentrations of L-CQD or lignin solutions, and the colors of L-CQD/PVA films and lignin/PVA films were compared. As the concentration of L-CQD (0, 0.125, 0.25, and 0.375 wt%) increases, the yellow color of the L-CQD/PVA film became more intense, with a corresponding increase in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity was measured using PL spectra (Fig. S6). The PVA film did not emit fluorescence when stimulated at 410 nm. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the L-CQD/PVA film increased proportionally with the amount of L-CQD, at emission wavelengths of 510 nm. Furthermore, it should be noted that the L-CQD/PVA film exhibited a significant degree of flexibility (Fig. 3a). At the same concentrations, the lignin/PVA films exhibited a considerably more intense shade of brown (Fig. S7). The utilization of the newly synthesized L-CQD results in a significant enhancement in the brightness of the films' color. Meanwhile, in order to broaden the application of L-CQD, additional polymers like polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polystyrene (PS), and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were used to create the films. However, despite using the same fabrication process, PMMA, PS, and PEG did not yield successful films. This is because PMMA and PS are insoluble in water-based solutions, while PEG has issues with moisture extraction. Nevertheless, films were successfully created using PVP. Under 365 nm UV light, the L-CQD/PVP film displayed green fluorescence, similar to that of the L-CQD/PVA film (Fig. S8).

By looking at cross-sectional scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the generated films (Fig. 3b), it was possible to figure out how thick they were. According to the L-CQD concentration (0, 0.125, 0.25, and 0.375 wt%), the film thicknesses were measured at 77 μm, 81 μm, 80 μm, and 78 μm, respectively, which are all quite similar. Moreover, the cross-sections of all films exhibited undamaged forms without any cracks or phase separation, and the surface of all films appeared to be consistently even and homogeneous. Additionally, to evaluate the impact of L-CQD concentration on the film's microstructure, uniformity, and surface roughness, surface images of the PVA and L-CQD/PVA films were obtained using field emission SEM (FE-SEM) at 10,000× and 30,000× magnifications (Fig. S9). Regardless of the L-CQD concentration, all film surfaces exhibited a high degree of smoothness, due to the incorporation of L-CQD with a size of around 8 nm into the PVA matrix. Surface roughness was further investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM) on L-CQD alone and on L-CQD/PVA films with various L-CQD concentrations (0, 0.125, 0.25, and 0.375 wt%) (Fig. S10). The root mean square (RMS) is a common metric used to quantify surface roughness [59,60]. The RMS of L-CQD was found to be 7–8 nm, consistent with the size seen in the TEM image. However, the RMS of the L-CQD/PVA film was <1 nm, indicating uniformly smooth surfaces across all films. Because the L-CQD was embedded in the PVA matrix, all films displayed uniform surfaces, irrespective of the L-CQD concentration. Since L-CQD was synthesized from lignin with –OH groups and EDA with –NH2 groups, the surface functional groups on L-CQD can form hydrogen bonds with the PVA matrix. The –OH and –NH stretching bands are very responsive to hydrogen bonding [22,61]. The PVA films exhibited a wide absorption band centered at 3273 cm−1, attributed to the symmetrical stretching vibration of –OH (Fig. S11). In the L-CQD/PVA film, the –OH/NH stretching band shifts to a higher wavenumber of 3274 cm−1 and is more pronounced compared to pure PVA [22]. The CO stretching band at 1707 cm−1 and the C=O/O-H band at 1083 cm−1 are also more evident in the L-CQD/PVA film than in PVA alone, indicating the presence of surface functional groups on L-CQDs and the occurrence of hydrogen bonding between L-CQD and PVA [61]. The homogeneous distribution of L-CQDs in the PVA matrix can be attributed to the hydrogen bonding interactions. Meanwhile, the absorbance peak at approximately 2910 cm−1 in the spectra of both the PVA and the L-CQD/PVA film represents the CH bonds in the PVA structure [61]. The peaks at 1429, 1322, and 840 cm−1 correspond to CH2 bending, OH stretching, and CC stretching, respectively, indicating the presence of PVA [62,63]. The PVA features dominate the spectrum of the L-CQD/PVA composite film since the composition of L-CQDs is only 0.375 wt% of PVA. IR analysis of L-CQD, PVA film, and L-CQD/PVA film suggests the formation of hydrogen bonds between L-CQD and PVA during the creation of the L-CQD/PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV/HEBL blocking performance of L-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the films' effectiveness in blocking UV and HEBL radiation, we analyzed the absorption and transmittance spectra of PVA film, L-CQD/PVA films, and lignin/PVA films (Fig. 4a, b, and Fig. S12). The absorption spectra revealed that the characteristic absorption peak of L-CQD at 420 nm increased in intensity as the concentration of L-CQD rose (Fig. 4a). This heightened absorption suggests that L-CQD can effectively and selectively absorb and block UV and HEBL. Based on the transmission spectra of the films (Fig. 4b), the PVA film without L-CQD transmitted 89.4 % of light at 550 nm, indicating high transparency. The transmittance of the L-CQD/PVA film gradually decreased in the UV and HEBL regions as the concentration of L-CQD increased. The blocking rates for UV-C, UV-B, UV-A, and HEBL regions in both the pure PVA film and the L-CQD/PVA film are presented in Table 1 . The pure PVA film showed blocking rates of 61.4 %, 30.2 %, 24.2 %, and 14.0 % in the UV-C, UV-B, UV-A, and HEBL regions, respectively. The L-CQD/PVA film's blocking rates for UV-A, UV-B, UV-C, and HEBL regions increased with rising L-CQD content. The 0.25 wt% L-CQD/PVA film blocked 100 % of UV-C, 100 % of UV-B, 99.5 % of UV-A, and 98.3 % of HEBL. Furthermore, the 0.375 wt% L-CQD/PVA film exhibited over 99.9 % HEBL blocking and complete 100 % UV blocking capabilities. In contrast, lignin/PVA films didn't show any significant absorption peak (Fig. S12a). The UV and HEBL protection efficacy of lignin/PVA films increased with higher lignin content, but it wasn't as effective as the L-CQD/PVA films (Fig. S12b). The 0.25 wt% lignin/PVA film blocked 94.6 % of UV-A and 72.2 % of HEBL, while the 0.375 wt% lignin/PVA film blocked 99.8 % of UV-A and 95.4 % of HEBL (Table S1).

There are numerous reports on UV-blocking films, but to the best of our knowledge, none exist on films that block both UV and HEBL completely. Therefore, it is essential to achieve high blocking efficiency in the HEBL region while maintaining a light tint and transparency in the visible spectrum. Fig. 4c and d depict the darkness and transparency of visible light and HEBL for L-CQD/PVA and lignin/PVA films, respectively. Under L-CQD concentration conditions where the HEBL was completely occluded, the L-CQD/PVA film showed 40 % obscurity and 51 % transparency in visible light at 550 nm, making it possible to clearly see leaves behind the film. In contrast, the lignin/PVA film with the same lignin concentration had a darkness level of 84 %, reduced visible light transparency (39 %), and a less effective HEBL blocking efficiency (95.4 %) than the L-CQD/PVA film. Moreover, it was observed that the green vegetation behind the lignin/PVA film was obscured. Consequently, the enhancement of lightness and transparency was achieved by the synthesis of L-CQD, successfully addressing the issues associated with lignin's dark color and low transparency. Furthermore, even a small quantity of L-CQD was highly effective at blocking UV and HEBL. While the yellow tint of the L-CQD/PVA film may limit its ability to accurately reproduce other colors and offer enough contrast, it can effectively mitigate photolysis or photo-oxidation [64–66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and mechanical characteristics of L-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the thermal stability of L-CQD/PVA films, the films were heated at 90 °C for 2 h. The L-CQD/PVA film with 0.375 wt% L-CQD exhibited no discernible change in dimensions, transparency, or shape even after this heat treatment (Fig. 5a). Additionally, the film's UV and HEBL blocking efficacy remained almost unchanged (Fig. 5b). To further assess the long-term thermal stability of the L-CQD/PVA film, transmittance spectra were measured (Fig. S13). The results indicated that even after exposure to 90 °C for a duration of 9 h, the transmission spectra experienced minimal changes. Therefore, the L-CQD/PVA film retained its ability to block UV and HEBL regions, even with prolonged exposure to heat. Moreover, the blocking effectiveness of the 0.375 wt% L-CQD/PVA film against UV and HEBL was tested after heating it above its melting point (Tm, 189.3 °C) (Table S2). Despite being heated to 200 °C for 2 min, the L-CQD/PVA film continued to effectively block UV and HEBL regions (Fig. S14). According to the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) curves (Fig. S15), the L-CQD/PVA film experiences three distinct phases of thermal decomposition: stage 1 from 50 to 140 °C, stage 2 from 230 to 470 °C, and stage 3 from 470 to 600 °C. During the initial phase, the film's weight decreases slightly due to moisture loss [67]. The pyrolysis behavior observed in stage 2 can be attributed to the breakdown of the main chain in PVA. While the maximum pyrolysis temperature (Tmax) of the 0.125 wt% L-CQD/PVA film was similar to that of the PVA film, the Tmax values for the 0.25 and 0.375 wt% L-CQD/PVA films were higher (Table S2). Since lignin undergoes slow thermal decomposition, it seems that the thermal decomposition peak of L-CQD/PVA films shifts to higher temperatures with increased L-CQD content [68,69]. Stage 3 is believed to arise from the carbonation process of the polymer composite, which is driven by an accompanying exothermic reaction [68,70]. At 700 °C, the PVA film underwent full decomposition. The residue percentages for the L-CQD/PVA films were 0.7 %, 4.8 %, and 7.8 %, depending on the increasing L-CQD content. The use of L-CQD enhanced flame retardancy and improved residue production efficiency in the film [71]. These results suggest that films containing L-CQD retain their UV and HEBL-blocking properties even after heat treatment, and that L-CQD positively impacts the films' thermal durability.

Additionally, the mechanical characteristics of the films were examined (Fig. S16). The PVA film has a tensile strength of 21.3 ± 0.8 MPa and a Young's modulus of 881.5 ± 5.5 MPa. As the L-CQD concentration increased, the L-CQD/PVA film's tensile strength and Young's modulus tended to rise. In particular, the film containing 0.375 wt% L-CQD had a tensile strength of 35.9 ± 1.5 MPa and a Young's modulus of 1083.3 ± 9.0 MPa. These results indicate that the tensile strength and Young's modulus were enhanced over the PVA film by 68.5 % and 22.9 %, respectively. Effective dispersion of L-CQDs in a PVA matrix can enhance mechanical performance [72]. Notably, the elongation at break of the L-CQD/PVA films decreased with increasing L-CQD concentration, likely due to the interaction between L-CQD and the PVA matrix, which restricts the movement of the matrix [73]. These results suggest that the incorporation of L-CQD into the PVA matrix strengthens the mechanical characteristic of the film. Also, their mechanical properties were investigated at a higher temperature, 90 °C. As the temperature increased, the tensile strength and Young's modulus of the films rose, while elongation at break decreased. The increase in tensile strength and Young's modulus seems to be due to increased crosslinking. In contrast, the decreased elongation at break may be related to reduced molecular flexibility and the transition to a more brittle state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effects of L-CQD/PVA film on UV-induced ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para: UV radiation directly affects cellular components or initiates ROS production through a photosensitizing mechanism [2]. Before assessing PVA and L-CQD/PVA films' effects on UV-induced ROS production, their photostability was examined by analyzing the transmittance spectra following 365 nm UV light irradiation with an intensity of 350 μW/cm2 (Fig. S17). The alteration in the transmittance spectra remained negligible even after exposure to UV radiation for 16 h. From the film's transmittance in the UV and HEBL zones as a function of UV irradiation time, the L-CQD/PVA film effectively obstructed the UV and HEBL regions despite prolonged exposure to 365 nm UV radiation. Besides, to examine the PL changes in the L-CQD/PVA film caused by exposure to outdoor light, the L-CQD/PVA film (0.375 wt%) was subjected to outdoor light for 12 h and its fluorescence intensity was assessed (Fig. S18). The results showed no significant differences in fluorescence intensity between the film exposed to outside light and that not exposed to external light. Next, to evaluate the capacity of the PVA and L-CQD/PVA films to suppress the production of ROS generated by UV radiation, we employed fluorescence-based detection techniques on two distinct cell types: human melanoma Hs 294T cells and human normal CCD-986sk cells (Fig. 6 ). In comparison to the Hs 294T control group without 254 nm UV exposure (100.00 ± 3.93 %), the UV-irradiated control group and the UV-irradiated cells coated with PVA film exhibited an increase in the rate of ROS generation of 145.06 ± 1.97 % and 143.00 ± 3.11 %, respectively (Fig. 6b). However, UV-irradiated cells covered with L-CQD/PVA film exhibited a mere 100.50 ± 0.73 % increase in the rate of ROS formation, indicating that the ROS production in this group closely resembles that of the control group without UV irradiation. These results show that the L-CQD/PVA film completely suppressed ROS generation by effectively blocking UV radiation at 254 nm. Likewise, 365 nm UV irradiation was employed (Fig. 6c). The ROS production rates of the 365 nm UV-irradiated control group and UV-irradiated cells covered with PVA film were 149.75 ± 3.38 % and 149.26 ± 3.90 %, respectively, compared to the non-UV-irradiated control group (100.00 %). In addition, the ROS production rate of UV-irradiated cells covered with L-CQD/PVA film was 103.59 ± 8.85 %, indicating suppression of ROS production comparable to that shown in Fig. 6b. The inhibitory effects of the L-CQD/PVA film on ROS formation in normal CCD-986sk cells were also investigated. The ROS production rates in control cells exposed to 254 nm UV and UV-irradiated cells covered with PVA film were found to be 147.51 ± 3.31 % and 146.72 ± 1.00 %, respectively. However, 254 nm UV-irradiated cells covered with the L-CQD/PVA film exhibited a notably reduced ROS production rate of 104.9 ± 1.21 %. Even under 365 nm UV irradiation, the CCD-986sk cells demonstrated the production of ROS similar to that of Hs 294T cells: UV-irradiated control (144.58 ± 5.69 %), with PVA film (140.52 ± 1.19 %), and with L-CQD/PVA film (105.31 ± 4.45 %). These results suggest that the L-CQD/PVA film successfully inhibited cellular ROS production by completely obstructing UV radiation. To investigate the effect of UV radiation on cell viability, we performed a cell viability assay using the same procedure as that for the ROS assay (Fig. S19). Both UV-irradiated cancer and normal cells, whether uncoated or coated with PVA film, demonstrated reduced cell viability owing to significantly elevated ROS generation. Nevertheless, the cell population that experienced a reduction in UV-induced ROS production due to the L-CQD/PVA film showed minimal cell mortality, comparable to that of the control cell group that was not subjected to UV light. Moreover, the rate of cell death induced by 254 nm UV was greater than that caused by 365 nm UV. The results showed that the L-CQD/PVA film efficiently inhibited ROS generation, thereby preventing cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blocking efficacy of L-CQD/PVA film against blue LD and blue LED
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the suitability of the L-CQD/PVA film as a blue light-blocking film, the extent of light blockage was investigated using a blue LD and LED. When exposed to 405 nm blue LD radiation, the PVA film did not obstruct the blue LD, resulting in the visibility of the transmitted blue light on the rear wall, similar to that of the blue LD (Fig. 7a). As the concentration of L-CQDs increased, the L-CQD/PVA film progressively reduced the number of blue LD transmitted to the rear wall. In particular, it was discovered that L-CQD/PVA films with a concentration of 0.375 wt% fully blocked the blue LD, resulting in the absence of any transmitted light through the rear wall. Similarly, after a 450 nm blue LED chip was enveloped by either a PVA or L-CQD/PVA film, we measured the transmitted light intensity (Fig. 7b). The intensity of blue light passing through the PVA film was similar to that of the blue LED employed as a reference. However, in the case of the L-CQD/PVA film, the 450 nm light was effectively obstructed. Furthermore, as the concentration of the L-CQDs increased, the emission of blue light into the surrounding environment gradually decreased. This reduction in the emission strength at 450 nm was also observed in the spectra, which aligns with the visual observations. These results indicate that the L-CQD/PVA film could efficiently block the HEBL region.

Finally, the HEBL-blocking efficacy and visible-light transparency of our L-CQD/PVA film were compared with those of previously published UV-blocking films (Fig. 8 ) [18,20–23,33,74–83]. The ideal integrated performance trends are indicated by diagonal arrows. The L-CQD/PVA film, specifically at a concentration of 0.125 wt% L-CQD, performed very well in blocking the HEBL at 450 nm with 90 % efficiency. Additionally, it maintained remarkable transparency, measuring 77 % at 550 nm. However, many studies have reported moderate degrees of HEBL-blocking efficiency and transparency. Several studies have demonstrated high HEBL-blocking efficacy of approximately 90 %; however, they suffer from poor transparency. Other investigations have showed a high level of transparency (approximately 80 %) but were ineffective in preventing HEBL. Thus, our L-CQD/PVA film with numerous benefits including highly efficient UV and HEBL blocking performance, good transparency, excellent photostability, thermal stability, suppression of UV-induced ROS generation, and complete obstruction of blue LD (405 nm) and blue LED (450 nm) has great potential as a UV and HEBL blocker for a variety of industrial applications, such as windows in cars and buildings, contact and optical lenses, sunscreens, UV-blocking fabrics, and digital electronic screens such as smartphones, tablets, liquid-crystal displays (LCDs), and LED light bulbs.

In this study, we successfully developed films that completely block UV and HEBL, using L-CQD synthesized from lignin, a woody biomass waste. To overcome lignin's inherent dark color and poor transparency, we synthesized L-CQD from lignin and EDA in an alkaline ethanol solution. Subsequently, we fabricated L-CQD/PVA films using the newly synthesized L-CQD. When compared to lignin/PVA films, the L-CQD/PVA film was 44 % lighter and 12 % more transparent in the visible light spectrum at 550 nm. Especially as L-CQD exhibits simultaneous UV and HEBL absorption capabilities, the L-CQD/PVA film provided complete UV and HEBL shielding. Additionally, the L-CQD/PVA film was thin, flexible, and thermally stable. To demonstrate its effectiveness, both cancerous and normal cell lines (Hs 294T, HeLa, CCD-986sk, and L929) were exposed to 254 nm and 365 nm UV light. The L-CQD/PVA film significantly inhibited cellular ROS formation by over 40 % by blocking UV light, compared to cells without a film or with a standard PVA film. Moreover, the L-CQD/PVA film successfully obstructed the transmission of blue LD and blue LED light by selectively filtering HEBL. These environmentally friendly, flexible, thermally stable, and easily fabricated L-CQD/PVA films have a lot of promise for use in numerous applications, including sunglasses and window films that block UV and HEBL.
